text: The robotic camera view shows a large right flank hernia with diastasis of the external oblique muscle. The internal and transversus abdominis muscles are disrupted, allowing partial herniation of the right lobe of the liver and the right kidney.
These are the preoperative coronal cuts.

The patient is positioned in the left lateral decubitus position. A 12mm supraumbilical trocar is inserted for intraperitoneal access, revealing dense adhesions from the right rectus muscle to the right flank during the initial laparoscopic evaluation. An 8mm subxiphoid trocar is placed, and the robotic system is docked over the right shoulder.

Following adhesiolysis of the anterior abdominal wall, the surgeon places an eight millimeter trocar in the right lower quadrant and a twelve millimeter trocar in the right mid quadrant. These placements allow for full visualization of the posterior aspect of the hernia defect. Single arm lysis is employed to clear the anterior abdominal wall, facilitating the insertion of accessory ports for further adhesiolysis.
The surgeon reduces the mesentery of the right colon and the kidney from the hernia defect, achieving complete reduction of the hernia contents.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum and creates a preperitoneal flap proximal to the medial aspect of the hernia defect, followed by the reduction of the hernia sac.
The posterior aspect of the hernia defect is visible, and the surgeon closes the defect primarily using an OV-lock suture.
The surgeon begins closing the posterior aspect of the hernia defect to offload tension over its widest portion, while reducing pneumoperitoneum to eight millimeters of mercury.

The surgeon completes the re-approximation of the transversus and internal oblique muscles. A 20x25 cm sheet of physio mesh is placed to reinforce the repair, secured to the abdominal wall with secure strap tackers. The peritoneal flap is re-approximated using tackers, with no mesh exposed.

The post-operative CT scan reveals an anterior seroma located behind the external oblique muscle and a pre-peritoneal seroma. The musculature remains approximated. Coronal cuts are displayed, with the lower left side showing the preoperative CT scan for comparison.
